user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: inc/main_callbacks.h
## My request for Codex:
I've made empty `src\main_callbacks.cpp` and empty `inc\main_callbacks.h`
for anything that is a directly registered callback function from `main.cpp`, move them there
agent_reply: Done. I moved the directly registered callback functions out of `main.cpp` into [src/main_callbacks.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp), with declarations in [inc/main_callbacks.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/main_callbacks.h).

I also updated [src/all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp) and [lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp) to include the new callback header instead of carrying local `extern` declarations. `main.cpp` now keeps the shared flow/helper functions and state that the callbacks use.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed. It only emitted the existing third-party `NetworkServer::available()` deprecation warning.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: src/main.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
const bool hasCloudDestinations = wifi_manager->cloudEndpointCount() > 0;
if (!hasCloudDestinations)
if (!show_wifi_sta_mode_view(false))
show_fatal_error_f(false, "Wi-Fi station view failed");
return;
## My request for Codex:
the highlighted logic here is wrong, because I forgot I had a NTP sync function as well, I can simply ask you to remove this, but intelligently so that it doesn't break anything
agent_reply: Fixed. In `handle_wifi_station_connected()` I removed the cloud-endpoint gate, so a successful station connection now always shows the connected Wi-Fi action `ScrollView`, which includes Information and Sync Time even with zero cloud upload destinations.
I also changed the Information action to call `show_wifi_sta_mode_view(true)` so the STA info view is dismissible when opened from that scroll menu.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed. PlatformIO touched `src/version.c`; I restored it so it stays out of the diff.

commit_message: code neatened, remove cloud features with a predefine

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: lib/AudioFileRecorder/DiskStats.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES
## My request for Codex:
clean up bugs I've introduced when I wrote in `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`
agent_reply: - Fixed a real overflow in `WifiManager::sanitize_network_config()` when cloud is disabled: it was iterating cloud entries using the station count.
- Made cloud-disabled builds use `CLOUD_SERVER_CNT_ALLOWED = 0` while keeping a legal one-slot storage array.
- Kept `DiskStats::refreshRecordingUploadStats()` scanning local recordings even when cloud upload is disabled.
- Made cloud-history pruning a clean no-op `Done` result when cloud is disabled.
- Cleaned `WebCfgHandlers` so disabled cloud config returns/parses as `cloud_uploads:false` without awkward `#ifdef` expression bugs.
- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed with cloud disabled.
- Temporarily enabled `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`, rebuilt `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, and it passed too.
- Restored the switch to commented-out afterward, and restored generated `src/version.c`.
- Fixed a real overflow in `WifiManager::sanitize_network_config()` when cloud is disabled: it was iterating cloud entries using the station count.
- Made cloud-disabled builds use `CLOUD_SERVER_CNT_ALLOWED = 0` while keeping a legal one-slot storage array.
- Kept `DiskStats::refreshRecordingUploadStats()` scanning local recordings even when cloud upload is disabled.
- Made cloud-history pruning a clean no-op `Done` result when cloud is disabled.
- Cleaned `WebCfgHandlers` so disabled cloud config returns/parses as `cloud_uploads:false` without awkward `#ifdef` expression bugs.
- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passed with cloud disabled.
- Temporarily enabled `BUILD_CLOUD_FEATURES`, rebuilt `pio run -e thefly_sec0`, and it passed too.
- Restored the switch to commented-out afterward, and restored generated `src/version.c`.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: platformio.ini
## My request for Codex:
inside the ScrollView for the Wi-Fi context, when clicking the back button and it causes a reset, first, blank out the screen as soon as possible
agent_reply: Done. In [main_callbacks.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:267), the Wi-Fi ScrollView back-button reset path now calls `thefly_display.fillScreen(TFT_BLACK);` before the reboot log/delay/restart.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`: build succeeded.

Done. In [main_callbacks.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:267), the Wi-Fi ScrollView back-button reset path now calls `thefly_display.fillScreen(TFT_BLACK);` before the reboot log/delay/restart.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`: build succeeded.

agent_reply: I tightened the Bluetooth connection path around that crash.

The key changes are in [BluetoothManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp:17): HFP now starts lazily, waits for the async HFP profile-ready event before calling `esp_hf_client_connect()`, resets those readiness flags on failure/deinit, and skips the unstable Bluedroid `AT+BAC` codec negotiation path. I also made pairing use Bluetooth/GAP only, then [main_callbacks.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:530) reboots after the pairing confirmation is dismissed so the later phone connection starts from a clean BT stack.

Build passes. Next test path: pair phone, dismiss confirmation, let The Fly reboot, then click the paired phone item and try connecting again. A remaining crash after that clean reboot would strongly point into the prebuilt ESP32 BT stack config, likely WBS/HFP codec support or verbose BT trace settings, rather than the app-side connection flow.

user_prompt: # Context from my IDE setup:
## Active file: src/main_callbacks.cpp
## Active selection of the file:
on_pairing_success_dialog_dismissed
## My request for Codex:
it's working, kind of, device to host and host to device connections both work

pairing is a bad experience, I think it looked like it rebooted but I didn't see any messages at all in my terminal, the serial port is working, uh, maybe the confirmation dialog registered the wrong return view so it returned to the splash screen instead of the mainscreenview, make sure that's not possible

the press to hold for deleting pairing does work but there's no indication that the user held for long enough. I need the trash-can icon to show up as soon as the timespan for a long-hold has been reached, instead of being checked only on release.
it feels like I have to delete a pair twice before it disappears from the list.

I want you to do something different, you have prevented a connection after a pair, this makes the phone very unhappy. I want to go back to the way it was before, when we can always accept an incoming connection. We simply don't start a recording. I see `on_pairing_success_dialog_dismissed` already issues a reboot. That's good.
agent_reply: Implemented and verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`.
- Bluetooth now starts full HFP at boot again, so incoming host connections can be accepted normally.
- Pairing no longer deinitializes HFP or makes the radio non-connectable.
- Pairing mode suppresses auto-recording immediately, so a phone can connect after pairing without starting a recording.
- Pairing success dialog now falls back to `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN` before reboot, so it cannot land on splash if reboot is delayed or interrupted.
- Bluetooth long-hold delete now shows the trash icon as soon as the hold threshold is reached.
- Unpair no longer immediately saves through the path that re-reads bond state too early, which should fix the “delete twice before it disappears” behavior.
Build passes. One note: `pio` regenerated `src/version.c` during the build.

user_prompt: ### Task: Playback
Help me implement `.wav` file playback

I have created `lib\WavPlayback\PlaybackView.cpp` and `lib\WavPlayback\WavPlayback.cpp` (and associated header files) to work with. `PlaybackView` should handle the GUI code as much as possible, while `WavPlayback` handles the reading of the file and the feeding of the audio into the speaker. Use `AudioManager` as much as possible. `PlaybackView` inherits from `FlyGuiView`
Clicking on a recorded file from the `ScrollView` will enter this view and start playback.
The duration of the file is an estimate based on file size, assuming 16 bit stereo PCM, and subtract the size of the RIFF header.
When playback is finished, go into pause mode (remember to change the icon).
### Scrub Bar

use `lib\WavPlayback\ScrubBar.cpp` and the associated header file to implement a scrubbing bar much like the `ProgressBar` in the sense that we can define a size and position of it. It also needs initialization parameters for total time. It needs setters for current position. It needs a callback that is inside `PlaybackView`  to handle touch events on the bar so that the `PlaybackView`  can delegate the actual file seek to `WavPlayback` and then call the update function of `ScrubBar`.
At the right side of screen, just above the `ScrubBar` itself, show the total file duration as “hh:mm:ss”

At the left side of the screen, just below the `ScrubBar` itself, show the current position in the file as “hh:mm:ss”, but in yellow text, and the same slightly bigger font used for the elapsed time during recording. Even if the code does something weird, do not let the displayed current time number exceed the estimated duration.
The bar itself is always blue, and since it is now capable of going backwards, the drawing of the bar needs to also draw a filled black rectangle after the filled blue rectangle
### The layout of the view
`sprite_record_50` in the top left corner, below the top bar. Beside this icon, show the file name, with a normal font, center aligned horizontally with the 50x50 icon
(note: I know we can fit 3 lines of text here)
The region from just below the icon, which I think is Y = 60, this is where we put a 300 pixel wide 12 pixel tall `ScrubBar` , horizontally centered

At the bottom of the screen will be three buttons, each should be centered as if the screen was divided in 3 columns and then the buttons centered in the columns. The icons are also tied to `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`
The left button is a play/pause toggle control. Use `sprite_playback_play_50` and `sprite_playback_pause_50` as appropriate

The center button is volume control, which cycles through 4 settings, use `sprite_speaker_50` for full volume, `sprite_speaker_66_50` for 66% volume, `sprite_speaker_33_50` for 33% volume, and `sprite_speaker_00_50` for 0% volume.
The right button is one that stops playback and quits, use `sprite_squarex_50`
### `WavPlayback`
This needs to open the file, get its size (based on file size, not RIFF data), estimate the duration.
Every pump action is 0.5 second worth of data. Pump the data into the `g_fifo_bt2spk`, we are borrowing it. Enable the speaker through `AudioManager` and we should get sound.
There are setters for playback position, which simply sets the file seek pointer intelligently.
A setter is needed for volume, which should use the volume function already implemented by `AudioManager`, I am pretty sure the application of the gain is already implemented

A setter is needed for the play/pause state, pausing means no more reading file content and enqueuing and clearing of the FIFO. The file seek pointer needs to be re-wound a bit according to how much of the FIFO was cleared.

The pump function I will call from almost the same place that `AudioFileRecorder::pump();` happens in `main.cpp`, because this module is like the opposite of `AudioFileRecorder` and I want it on the same thread/core for the same reasons (SPI sharing for LCD screen and microSD card)

`WavPlayback` is started when `PlaybackView` is loaded, and stopped (file handle closed) when unloaded. I am pretty sure by doing it this way will mean that an incoming bluetooth connection will automatically stop the playback, close the file, and start a recording, which is great.
### Future Steps
At this point, you can probably hand me something that is immediately testable. I already have a `.wav` file on the card.
I will tweak the UI, ask for bug fixes, and then see if I need to add more precise rewind and fast forwarding buttons.
agent_reply: - Added `WavPlayback` engine in [WavPlayback.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/WavPlayback.cpp): opens WAV, estimates duration from file size minus 44-byte RIFF header, seeks, pauses with FIFO rewind, sets volume, and pumps audio into `AudioManager::bluetoothToSpeakerFifo()`.
- Added `PlaybackView` in [PlaybackView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WavPlayback/PlaybackView.cpp): file title, scrub bar, play/pause, 4-step volume, stop/exit.
- Wired `.wav` clicks from `ScrollView` into playback using the selected filename.
I also made playback mix stereo to mono before feeding the speaker FIFO, so overlapping mic/BT channels won’t silently drop the right channel.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec0` passes. One intentional simplification: WAV parsing assumes the generated files are 16 kHz, stereo, 16-bit PCM with a 44-byte header, exactly as requested.

user_prompt: ### Task: Pin Pad
I’ve created blank files for you to work with, `lib\PinCode\PinPadView.cpp` and its associated header file. This should inherit from `FlyGuiView`

The configuration should include a string, that is the target correct pin code in ASCII, don’t worry about validating it. The configuration also includes three callbacks: on-success, on-failed-attempt, and on-exit.
if the pin code is empty, then the rule is, there’s simply never a success. the pin code shall be set to empty if the pin code has been revoked

on the bottom of the screen, divide the space up as if it had 3 columns. in the center of the left column, put the text “CLEAR”. in the center of the right column, put the text “EXIT”. Use font 2. These two functions are tied to `BtnA` and `BtnC`

on the top of the screen, 2 pixels below the top bar area, is reserved for a line 2 pixels thick. For every character entered, this line grows from left to right. The length of the actual pin code determines how big each segment is. So the last char entered causes it to fill the whole width of the screen.

2 pixels below that line to 2 pixels below the text on the bottom, is a 3x3 grid for numbers 1 to 9, arranged with 1 in the top left corner. to implement this, create a class called `PinNumBtn` that inherits from `FlyGuiView`. It has a number associated, an inactive border, and a touched border. The inactive border color will be grey. When arranged, a 3x3 grey grid is formed. When held, the border turns yellow instead of white, when released, the border turns grey again.
when a button is pressed, a function is called into the `PinPadView` to register the entry. entering the last char causes an immediate evaluation of the contents to see if there is a match
look, if this works for my iPad, this works here, ok?

if there is no match, that line that has been growing is redrawn as red, and then from right to left, in every redraw call, draw some black so that it looks like it is receding towards the left (the size dictates the rate). This is the anti-brute force cooldown, so do not allow the interface to react during this time. The time of this cooldown is about 2 seconds for the first three attempts, about 5 seconds for all other attempts. Every bad attempt has a callback. Eventually we will wire up this bad attempt callback to register absolute bad attempt count and revoke the pin.
on-exit, simply return to the previous view

for now, wire it up with a pin of `123456` for testing, activate it only if entering `ScrollView` for the file list from the `MainScreenView` while the build has `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` set to 1. So on success, show the `ScrollView` it was originally supposed to show
I will likely tweak font sizes, and maybe the grid becomes 4x2 or 3x2 depending on what it looks like and how accurately I can touch the keys.
agent_reply: Added the new view in [PinPadView.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/PinCode/PinPadView.h:38) and [PinPadView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/PinCode/PinPadView.cpp:179), including the 3x3 numeric grid, CLEAR/EXIT button handling, progress line, failed-attempt cooldown animation, empty-pin-never-success behavior, and callbacks.

Wired it into the GUI via [FlyGui.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.h:55), [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:55), and [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:274). The MainScreenView file-list path now gates through pin `123456` only when `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL == 1`, then opens the existing `ScrollView` on success in [main_callbacks.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:191).
Only warning was the existing third-party ESP-FTP-Server deprecated `available()` warning.

user_prompt: I read your thoughts and realized I may have provided wrong instructions about NVS partitioning
Write code in `lib\PinCode\PinCode.cpp` and its associated header file
Use the same NVS namespace that `Aegis` uses, with just one new item for the single `pin_code_cfg_t` instance of data, which is read and held in memory.
The initialization function will read that data out or create it as all zero data if it doesn’t exist in NVS.
Functions that need to exist:
- log bad attempt, increments `bad_attempts` and see if it exceeds `PIN_CODE_MAX_BAD_ATTEMPTS_ALLOWED`, in which case, revoke the pin, save to NVS
- log successful usage, resets `bad_attempts` , save to NVS
- revoke pin, which sets the boolean `active` to false and logs the time in `date_revoked` , save to NVS
- set the pin to a string (customization), mark it as `active` and log the date `date_set` and reset the salt to 0 , save to NVS

- regenerate the pin, increment the `salt`, then make a hash using the filecrypt-key and the `salt` , then deterministically turn the hash into a series of digits (length is `PINCODE_DEFAULT_LENGTH`) that only uses numbers 1 to 9 , save to NVS
- getter for the pin, return null if not active
The preprocessor define `TEST_MOCK_PIN_CODE` will make the pin code getter always return `123456`
The custom pin setting function is only available for `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL` as 1.
Remove the configuration of the pin code from the `PinPadView`, it should instead internally fetch the pin using this new `PinCode.cpp`
The initialization of `PinCode.cpp` is called from within `Aegis::init()`
Any writing of `time_t` members will use `ClockAgent`
agent_reply: - Stores one `pin_code_cfg_t` blob named `pin_code_cfg` in the existing `aegis` NVS namespace.
- `Aegis::init()` now initializes `PinCode` too, including already-initialized paths.
- `PinPadView` no longer accepts a configured target PIN; it fetches from `PinCode::getPin()`, logs success/failure, and uses persisted bad-attempt count.

Small note: `TEST_MOCK_PIN_CODE` is still controlled by `inc/conf.h`; with it disabled and no stored active PIN yet, the getter correctly returns `nullptr`, so the PIN pad has no success path until `setPin()` or `regeneratePin()` is used.
